flyers.title: 2026 Realtor Cheat Sheet
flyers.blurb: One-page snapshot of loan limits, down payments, seller contributions, and seasoning requirements across Conventional, FHA, VA, and USDA. The fastest answer to “can my buyer qualify?”
flyers.use_when: Pull this up the moment a buyer question comes up.

flyers.title: 2026 Loan Cheat Sheet
flyers.blurb: Deeper side-by-side of all five loan types — credit scores, down payments, MI, manufactured-home rules, and the property-condition gotchas (handrails, well/septic, road maintenance) that derail closings.
flyers.use_when: When a property’s condition or a buyer’s profile is unusual.

flyers.title: 2026 Quick Loan Reference Guide
flyers.blurb: Occupancy, down payment, MI, DTI, and minimum credit by program, plus 2026 conforming and FHA loan limits by unit count. Includes bankruptcy and foreclosure waiting periods.
flyers.use_when: Quick limit and DTI lookups for any scenario.

flyers.title: Product Reference Guide
flyers.blurb: The full menu of CMG programs in one place — from All In One Loan and HomeFundIt to bank-statement, 1099-only, asset-qualifier, reverse, and builder tools. A map of everything I can do.
flyers.use_when: When you’re not sure a program exists — it probably does.

flyers.title: Closing Costs Explained
flyers.blurb: A plain-English rundown of the common closing costs — origination, appraisal, title/escrow, recording, daily interest, and the tax and insurance escrows. Great for setting buyer expectations early.
flyers.use_when: Buyers asking “what are all these fees?”

flyers.title: The Three-Day Disclosure Rule
flyers.blurb: A visual chart of the TRID 3-day rule — by closing day, when the buyer must receive the Closing Disclosure and when it must be mailed. Pairs with the CD timing tool below.
flyers.use_when: Planning a closing timeline.

flyers.title: Loan Document Checklist
flyers.blurb: Everything a buyer needs to gather, split for salaried/hourly and self-employed borrowers — IDs, pay stubs, W2s/1099s, tax returns, and asset statements. Speeds up the whole process.
flyers.use_when: Send the moment a buyer is ready to apply.

flyers.title: CMG Community ONE — Down Payment Assistance
flyers.blurb: A 2% grant up to $6,000 with down payments as low as 1%, paired with HomeReady or Home Possible. Built to get more first-time buyers to the closing table.
flyers.use_when: First-time and budget-conscious buyers.

flyers.title: CMG Graduate Advantage
flyers.blurb: Recent grads in medical, legal, engineering, architecture, or accounting can qualify on future income with a signed offer letter — up to 90% financing and $1.5M, no current income needed.
flyers.use_when: New professionals with a job offer but little history.

flyers.title: CMG MedPro — No Down Payment for Doctors
flyers.blurb: No down payment and no mortgage insurance for eligible physicians, dentists, vets, CRNAs, residents, and fellows. One occupying borrower must be practicing in their field.
flyers.use_when: Medical professionals — a strong relocation niche.

flyers.title: Not Approved? Let’s Take a Second Look
flyers.blurb: A turned-down buyer isn’t a dead lead. Covers our full loan menu plus Texas DPA — HomeFundIt, TSAHC, TDHCA, FHA Buyer’s Choice, and MedPro — for buyers another lender passed on.
flyers.use_when: Any buyer who heard “no” somewhere else.

flyers.title: DSCR Loans for Investors
flyers.blurb: Qualify on the property’s rental income — no W2s or tax returns. Seven DSCR options up to $3M and 85% LTV for single-family, multi-unit, and short-term rentals.
flyers.use_when: Investor clients building a portfolio.

flyers.title: Renovation Lending Guide
flyers.blurb: Turn a dated listing into a buyer’s dream. Full breakdown of FHA 203(k), Limited 203(k), HomeStyle, and VA Renovation — how each works, what’s allowed, and how draws are funded.
flyers.use_when: Hard-to-move listings or buyers who want to customize.

flyers.title: List & Lock
flyers.blurb: A modern alternative to price cuts and buydowns. Sellers earn a credit up to $5,000, buyers lock a lower rate, and CMG kicks in an extra 1%. A listing-appointment differentiator.
flyers.use_when: Listing presentations and rate-sensitive buyers.

flyers.title: Cash Offer Programs
flyers.blurb: Help buyers compete like cash with Knock, Zavvie, and Zoom Casa — bridge loans, all-cash offers on the next home, and buy-before-you-sell options. Includes program comparisons and coverage.
flyers.use_when: Buyers who need to win a bidding war or move before selling.

flyers.title: Conventional Condo Financing Updates
flyers.blurb: Fannie/Freddie loosened condo insurance and project-review rules in 2026 — more condo projects now qualify for conventional financing with as little as 3% down.
flyers.use_when: Condo deals that stalled on project eligibility.

flyers.title: Why Homeownership Builds Wealth
flyers.blurb: 80+ years of U.S. home-price history — a 71-6-5 record since 1944 — to reassure buyers waiting for a crash that never comes. Great share-piece for fence-sitters.
flyers.use_when: Buyers paralyzed by “prices have to drop.”

flyers.title: Buying a Home for a Parent or Disabled Child
flyers.blurb: A parent or adult child can buy a home as a primary residence for a low/no-income family member — with owner-occupied rates and terms, not investment pricing.
flyers.use_when: A client wants to house an aging parent or a disabled adult child.
highlights: Conventional (Fannie Mae & Freddie Mac) and FHA all allow it: a borrower can purchase a home for a parent or disabled child who can't qualify on their own, and it's treated as a primary residence — so owner-occupied LTV, rates, and terms apply instead of investment-property pricing.
The family member who will live there generally does NOT have to be on the loan for Conventional. The occupying relative just needs to use it as their primary residence.
FHA is different: the parent or disabled child must be on the loan, with the buyer as a non-occupant co-borrower. If the occupant has no credit score, FHA requires manual underwriting.
VA and USDA do not offer this — it's a Conventional and FHA play only.

Documentation is light on the Conventional side: lenders may ask for a short letter stating the purchase is intended as the relative's primary residence, but extensive proof of income shortfall generally isn't required.
flyers.footer_note: General guideline overview, not a commitment to lend — agency rules change and each file is unique. Reach out and I'll confirm the best structure for your client's situation.

flyers.title: Condo Financing Just Got Easier in 2026
flyers.blurb: Fannie and Freddie loosened condo insurance and project-review rules this year. More condo deals now qualify for conventional financing.
flyers.use_when: A condo deal stalled on project eligibility.
highlights: Updated insurance and project-review standards mean more condo projects clear conventional review than before.
Some buyers can now get in with as little as 3% down on eligible condo projects.
A warrantability check upfront is still smart — send me the project and I will confirm before your buyer writes.
flyers.footer_note: General overview, not a commitment to lend. Each project is reviewed individually.

flyers.title: Keeping the Old House? How Rental Income Helps Your Buyer Qualify
flyers.blurb: When a buyer keeps their current home and rents it out to buy the next one, the rent can help them qualify — but how much counts depends on the loan type and their landlord experience. Here's the plain-English version.
flyers.use_when: A buyer wants to keep their current home as a rental and buy a new primary residence.
highlights: The setup: your buyer keeps their current home, rents it out, and buys a new primary residence. The rent on the departing home can offset that home's payment — and sometimes add to qualifying income — which can make or break the new loan's debt-to-income.

Conventional (Fannie & Freddie): a current signed lease is required, plus proof it is real (recent rent deposits, or the security deposit and first month rent). Generally 75% of the lease rent is used to offset the departing home full payment (principal, interest, taxes, insurance, HOA).

The experience rule on Conventional: if your buyer has at least 1 year of landlord/property-management experience, rent above the departing home's payment can be added to their income. Without that experience, the rent can only cancel out the payment — no extra income credit.

FHA: a fully executed 1-year lease plus proof of the security deposit or first month rent. To use the rent, the buyer generally needs to be relocating 100+ miles, hold at least 25% equity (shown by a full appraisal), and full rent can offset the payment only — no reserves required.
VA: full rental income can be used to offset the departing home's payment, and VA does not require proof of property-management experience.
USDA: not an option here — USDA requires a 2-year history of receiving rental income, so a freshly converted departing residence will not qualify.
Short-term rentals (Airbnb-style): a brand-new short-term lease without a 12-month Schedule E history generally cannot be used on the Conventional side.
flyers.footer_note: General guideline overview, not a commitment to lend — agency rules change and every file is different. Send me the scenario and I'll confirm exactly how the numbers work for your client.
